# Heartwood

> Nostr private keys are pasted into web apps, stored in browser extensions, and copied to the clipboard. Heartwood keeps them on dedicated hardware instead.

Heartwood is open-source Nostr signing built on nsec-tree. `heartwood-bridge` is a headless, keyless daemon that connects Nostr relays to a USB-tethered hardware signer -- an ESP32 running the [heartwood-esp32](https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood-esp32) firmware. The bridge holds no key material and never sees plaintext: NIP-44 decryption, NIP-46 request dispatch, per-client permissions, session/replay protection, and signing all happen on the device itself, inline over USB. The bridge is only the device's relay connection, and nothing more.

It is aimed at Nostr power users who want hardware-backed key security without renting a VPS, and at app developers building multi-identity Nostr clients that need a signing backend which works on iOS, Android, and web equally, since NIP-46 is relay-mediated.

Heartwood is a direct replacement for nsecBunker (no VPS required), an iOS-compatible alternative to Amber (Android-only), and a multi-identity alternative to LNbits NSD (one key per device). The distinguishing feature is unlinkable-by-default multi-identity: one seed on the hardware signer derives unlimited separate identities that cannot be correlated by an observer unless you deliberately prove the link.

## Getting Started

Two pieces: a hardware signer (an ESP32 running `heartwood-esp32`, with keys provisioned over USB via [Sapwood](https://sapwood.forgesworn.dev) or the offline `provision` CLI) and a bridge host (any small Linux box with a USB port and a network connection -- a Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W or similar).

Install the bridge:

```bash
curl -sL https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood/releases/latest/download/install.sh | sudo bash
```

Point it at the signer's serial port and start it:

```bash
# /etc/systemd/system/heartwood-bridge.service
#   Environment=HEARTWOOD_SERIAL_PORT=/dev/ttyUSB0
sudo systemctl start heartwood-bridge
```

The `bridge.secret` file -- written onto the box over USB by Sapwood or the `provision` CLI -- must be present in `HEARTWOOD_DATA_DIR` (default `/var/lib/heartwood`) before the bridge will start. See [docs/QUICKSTART.md](docs/QUICKSTART.md) for the full walkthrough, including Docker.

Pair a Nostr client with the `bunker://` connection string shown in Sapwood. There is no web UI or QR code served by this repo; pairing and per-client permissions are configured on the device itself.

## Key Concepts

- **TreeRoot** — the master secret derived from a 12-word BIP-39 mnemonic at path `m/44'/1237'/727'/0'/0'`. Lives only on the hardware signer.
- **Identity** — a child key derived from TreeRoot via HMAC-SHA256 keyed on `(purpose, index)`. Each purpose/index pair produces a deterministic, unique keypair.
- **Persona** — a named identity at `nostr:persona:{name}`. Convenience wrapper around `derive(root, "nostr:persona:{name}", index)`.
- **NIP-46** — the Nostr remote signing protocol used to pair clients with the hardware signer. Method dispatch, permissions, and session handling happen on the device (firmware in `heartwood-esp32`), not in this repo.
- **Linkage proof** — a BIP-340 Schnorr signature from the master key proving ownership of a child key, without revealing the derivation path (blind) or with it revealed (full).

## API Surface (Rust crates)

### heartwood-core (crate name `nsec-tree-rs`)

```rust
use nsec_tree_rs::{from_mnemonic, from_nsec, npub_from_nsec, derive,
                   derive_from_identity, derive_persona, derive_from_persona,
                   create_blind_proof, create_full_proof, verify_proof,
                   recover, encode_npub, encode_nsec, decode_npub, decode_nsec};

// Create a root from a 12-word mnemonic
let root = from_mnemonic("word1 word2 ... word12", None)?;

// Derive a child identity
let identity = derive(&root, "social", 0)?;
println!("{}", identity.npub);   // npub1...
println!("{}", identity.nsec()); // nsec1...

// Derive a named persona
let persona = derive_persona(&root, "work", None)?;

// Derive a sub-identity from an existing identity
let sub = derive_from_identity(&identity, "notifications", 0)?;

// Derive a sub-identity from a persona
let sub = derive_from_persona(&persona, "dms", None)?;

// Get the npub for a bare nsec (bunker mode -- no tree derivation)
let npub = npub_from_nsec("nsec1...")?;

// Prove ownership of a child key (blind = no purpose/index revealed)
let proof = create_blind_proof(&root, &identity)?;
assert!(verify_proof(&proof)?);

// Recover identities by scanning purposes
let found = recover(&root, &["social".to_string(), "work".to_string()], None)?;
```

This crate is a standalone reference/compatibility library now -- it is no longer a dependency of `heartwood-bridge` or of any binary in this repo. The same HMAC-SHA256 derivation scheme runs on-device, in the `heartwood-esp32` firmware.

## NIP-46 Integration (client side)

Any standard NIP-46 (Nostr Connect) client can pair with the hardware signer via the relay named in its `bunker://` or `nostrconnect://` URI -- Heartwood does not change the wire protocol clients speak. There is no Tor hidden service and no web UI; pairing, client approval, and per-client permissions are all configured on the device itself, normally via [Sapwood](https://sapwood.forgesworn.dev).

**Compatible clients:** any standard NIP-46 client. [Bark](https://github.com/forgesworn/bark) is a browser extension providing NIP-07 signing via Heartwood, built specifically for this stack.

## Security Properties

- Keys derived via HMAC-SHA256 (RFC 2104) -- not BIP-32 extended keys at the child level
- Master secret zeroed from RAM on drop (`zeroize` crate), intermediate secrets explicitly zeroised
- The bridge holds no key material and never sees plaintext -- NIP-44 decryption, NIP-46 dispatch, and signing all happen on the USB-tethered hardware signer, not the bridge
- Per-client kind restrictions, connection approval, and rate limiting are enforced on the device
- Session handling and replay protection for requests are the device's responsibility, not the bridge's
- NIP-46 messages are NIP-44 encrypted end-to-end between client and device
- `bridge.secret` authenticates the USB serial session only -- it is not a signing key; treat the file as a local secret (0600 permissions)
- No inbound listener -- the bridge only makes outbound WebSocket connections to relays; no open ports, no Tor hidden service
- NIP-42 AUTH is unsupported -- the bridge holds no key, so it cannot sign an auth challenge
- 59 tests in heartwood-core (`nsec-tree-rs`), including 5 frozen protocol vectors cross-validated against the TypeScript reference implementation

## Competitors

| Project | Platform | Multi-identity | Hardware |
|---------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **Heartwood** | Hardware signer + bridge | Yes (unlimited, unlinkable) | Yes |
| nsecBunker | VPS/server | No | No |
| Amber | Android only | No | No |
| nsec.app | Browser extension | No | No |
| LNbits NSD | Raspberry Pi | One key only | Yes |

## Ecosystem

- [nsec-tree](https://github.com/forgesworn/nsec-tree) — TypeScript reference implementation of the derivation protocol
- [shamir-words](https://github.com/forgesworn/shamir-words) — mnemonic threshold backup (Phase 2 integration)
- [canary-kit](https://github.com/forgesworn/canary-kit) — dead man's switch (pairs with Heartwood duress persona)
- [bark](https://github.com/forgesworn/bark) — browser extension for NIP-07 signing via Heartwood
- [bray](https://github.com/forgesworn/bray) — Nostr MCP server (AI agents sign through Heartwood)

## Source

For the full API reference, see [llms-full.txt](./llms-full.txt).
